Lecture 8: Cross-Filling Projections ​

Topics: §3.2–3.3 — Cross-Filling Decomposition of Projections, VU = I Property, rank(P) = trace(P), Rank-1 Projection Characterization, Full-Rank Invertibility Date: Apr 6 – Apr 9, 2026

Introduction ​

In Lecture 7, we introduced projections through the sunlight-floor model and proved basic properties from P2=P. We also characterized orthogonal projections by the symmetry condition P=PT.

Now we explore the internal structure of projections.

::: question What Makes Projections Special?

Any matrix can be cross-filled: A=R1+R2+⋯+Rr (sum of rank-1 pieces). For a general matrix, the pieces Ri are just arbitrary rank-1 matrices.

What happens when we cross-fill a projection? :::

1. The Remarkable Discovery ​

1.1 Review: Two Forms of Cross-Filling ​

Recall from Lecture 3 that cross-filling decomposes any matrix into:

Sum form: A=R1+R2+⋯+Rr where each Ri has rank 1 and r=rank(A).

Product form: A=UV where U is m×r (columns of U are the column parts of each Ri), V is r×n (rows of V are the row parts of each Ri).

The two forms are related: Ri=uiviT where ui is column i of U and viT is row i of V.

1.2 Cross-Filling a General Matrix ​

Example

Example 1.1: A Non-Projection Matrix

A=(2412)

This has rank 1. Cross-filling gives:

A=(21)(12)=UV

So U=(21) and V=(12).

Compute VU:

VU=(12)(21)=4

Note: VU=4≠1=I1. Also, A2=(81648)≠A.

For general matrices, VU is just some number or matrix — nothing special.

1.3 Cross-Filling a Projection ​

Now let's try the same process with projection matrices.

Example

Example 1.2: A Rank-1 Projection

P=15(1224)

Verify: P2=125(1224)(1224)=125(5101020)=15(1224)=P ✓

Cross-filling at pivot (1,1) with value 15:

P=(12)⋅15⋅(12)=UV

So U=(12) and V=15(12).

Compute VU:

VU=15(12)(12)=15(1+4)=1=I1

Remarkable! For this projection, VU=I.

Also notice: rank(P)=1 and trace(P)=15(1+4)=1. So rank(P)=trace(P).

Is this just a coincidence? Let's try a higher-rank example.

Example

Example 1.3: A Rank-2 Projection

P=(000−110−101)

Verify: P2=(000−110−101)(000−110−101)=(000−110−101)=P ✓

This has rank(P)=2. Cross-fill at pivot (2,2) with value 1:

R1=(010)(−110)=(000−110000)

Remainder: P−R1=(000000−101). Cross-fill at pivot (3,3):

R2=(001)(−101)=(000000−101)

Check: Are the rank-1 pieces projections?

R12=(000−110000)(000−110000)=(000−110000)=R1✓R22=(000000−101)(000000−101)=(000000−101)=R2✓

Check: Are they mutually annihilating?

R1R2=(000−110000)(000000−101)=(000000000)✓

Check: Product form and VU.

U=(001001),V=(−110−101)VU=(−110−101)(001001)=(1001)=I2✓

Again: VU=I and every summand is a projection!

Also: trace(P)=0+1+1=2=rank(P).

1.4 The Pattern ​

Attention

The Discovery

When we cross-fill a projection P=R1+R2+⋯+Rr:

  1. Every rank-1 piece is automatically a projection: Ri2=Ri for all i
  2. All pieces are mutually annihilating: RiRj=0 for i≠j
  3. Rank equals trace: rank(P)=trace(P)

None of this happens for general matrices. The equation P2=P forces extraordinary structure.

Why does this happen? The key is hidden in the product form.

2. The Proof Mechanism: P=UV Implies VU=I ​

2.1 Statement ​

Proposition

Theorem 2.1 (VU = I Automatic)

Let P be a projection (P2=P). Suppose P=UV where:

  • U is n×r with full column rank (rank r)
  • V is r×n with full row rank (rank r)

Then VU=Ir automatically.

Remark

Role of This Theorem

This is not the discovery — it is the proof mechanism that explains the discovery. We need VU=I as a tool to prove that cross-filling projections produces rank-1 projections.

2.2 Proof ​

The proof uses only P2=P and the cancellation properties from Lecture 4.

Step 1: Expand P2=P using P=UV:

UVUV=UV

Step 2: Left-cancel U.

Since U has full column rank, its columns are linearly independent. Therefore U is left-cancelable:

UVUV=UV⟹VUV=V

Step 3: Right-cancel V.

Since V has full row rank, its rows are linearly independent. Therefore V is right-cancelable:

VUV=V⟹VU=Ir

Done! ∎

2.3 Completing the Proof of the Discovery ​

Now we can explain why cross-filling a projection produces rank-1 projections.

Write P=UV with VU=Ir. The rank-1 pieces from cross-filling are:

Ri=uiviT

where ui is column i of U and viT is row i of V.

The condition VU=Ir means:

viTuj={1if i=j0if i≠j

This single fact proves everything:

Each Ri is a projection:

Ri2=(uiviT)(uiviT)=ui(viTui)viT=ui⋅1⋅viT=Ri✓

Pieces are mutually annihilating:

For i≠j:

RiRj=(uiviT)(ujvjT)=ui(viTuj)vjT=ui⋅0⋅vjT=0✓

Remark

The Trace Connection

From viTui=1, we also get:

trace(Ri)=trace(uiviT)=viTui=1

Each rank-1 piece has trace exactly 1. Summing over all pieces:

trace(P)=∑i=1rtrace(Ri)=r=rank(P)

This leads to the next major theorem.

3. Rank Equals Trace for Projections ​

3.1 The Theorem ​

Proposition

Theorem 3.1 (Rank = Trace for Projections)

For any projection P (satisfying P2=P):

rank(P)=trace(P)

Proof:

Write P=UV where rank(P)=r. By Theorem 2.1, VU=Ir.

Using the cyclic property of trace:

trace(P)=trace(UV)=trace(VU)=trace(Ir)=r=rank(P)

Done! ∎

3.2 Why Only for Projections ​

Remark

This Identity Is Special to Projections

For a general matrix A=UV, we still have trace(A)=trace(UV)=trace(VU). But VU is NOT the identity, so we cannot conclude trace(A)=rank(A).

Compare:

  • Example 1.1 (not a projection): A=(2412), rank=1, trace=4. Different!
  • Example 1.2 (projection): P=15(1224), rank=1, trace=1. Equal!

The identity rank=trace is a fingerprint of projection matrices.

This identity now opens up three important branches.

4. Three Consequences ​

4.1 Rank-1 Projection Characterization ​

Proposition

Corollary 4.1 (Rank-1 Projection Characterization)

For a square matrix A, consider the following three conditions:

  1. rank(A)=1
  2. trace(A)=1
  3. A2=A (projection)

If any two of these conditions hold, then all three hold.

Proof:

(1) + (2) ⟹ (3): If rank(A)=1 and trace(A)=1.

Write A=uvT (rank 1). Then trace(A)=vTu=1.

A2=(uvT)(uvT)=u(vTu)vT=u⋅1⋅vT=A✓

(1) + (3) ⟹ (2): If rank(A)=1 and A2=A.

Write A=UV with U being n×1 and V being 1×n. By Theorem 2.1: VU=I1=1.

trace(A)=trace(UV)=trace(VU)=1✓

(2) + (3) ⟹ (1): If trace(A)=1 and A2=A.

By Theorem 3.1: rank(A)=trace(A)=1. ✓

Done! ∎

Remark

Why Is This Useful?

To check whether a rank-1 matrix is a projection, we only need to compute its trace. If the trace is 1, the projection property A2=A is automatic.

4.2 Key Lemma: UV=I Forces Square Matrices ​

Here is a surprising rigidity result.

Proposition

Corollary 4.2 (UV=I Forces Square)

Suppose U is n×r and V is r×n with UV=In and r≤n.

Then r=n, and both U and V are square invertible matrices with VU=UV=In.

Proof:

Step 1: VU is a projection.

(VU)(VU)=V(UV)U=VInU=VU

So VU is an r×r projection.

Step 2: Ir−VU is also a projection.

(Ir−VU)2=Ir−2VU+(VU)2=Ir−2VU+VU=Ir−VU

Step 3: Compute the trace.

trace(Ir−VU)=r−trace(VU)=r−trace(UV)=r−trace(In)=r−n

Step 4: Apply rank = trace.

Since Ir−VU is a projection, by Theorem 3.1:

rank(Ir−VU)=r−n

Since rank≥0: r≥n. Combined with r≤n: r=n.

Step 5: Conclude.

With r=n: rank(Ir−VU)=0, so Ir−VU=0, giving VU=In.

Therefore both U and V are n×n with UV=VU=In. ∎

Remark

Geometric Meaning

You cannot have a "thin" matrix times a "fat" matrix equal the identity. If UV=I, both factors must be square and invertible. The identity matrix is too "rigid" to be factored into mismatched dimensions.

An important special case:

Proposition

Proposition 4.3 (One-Sided Inverse ⟹ Two-Sided for Square Matrices)

If F and V are both n×n and FV=In, then VF=In.

Proof: Apply Corollary 4.2 with r=n: from FV=In (with F being n×n and V being n×n), we get VF=In. ∎

Remark

Comparison with Lecture 1

In Lecture 1, we defined "invertible" by assuming both AB=I and BA=I. Proposition 4.3 shows that for square matrices, one equation suffices:

FV=In⟹VF=In

This is a strictly stronger result: we no longer need to assume the existence of a two-sided inverse.

4.3 Full-Rank Square Matrix Is Invertible ​

Proposition

Theorem 4.4 (Full Rank ⟺ Invertible)

An n×n matrix X is invertible if and only if rank(X)=n.

Proof:

(⟹) Invertible ⟹ full rank:

Suppose XY=In for some Y. Cross-fill X=UV where U is n×k, V is k×n, and k=rank(X)≤n.

Then:

U(VY)=In

where U is n×k and VY is k×n with k≤n.

By Corollary 4.2: k=n. Therefore rank(X)=n. ✓

(⟸) Full rank ⟹ invertible:

Suppose rank(X)=n. We construct an explicit inverse.

Step 1: Cross-fill X=UV where U and V are both n×n (since rank =n, the decomposition uses n rank-1 pieces).

Step 2: Augmented cross-filling (horizontal).

Apply cross-filling to the augmented matrix (X∣In) using the same pivot selections as in Step 1.

At each step, the pivot lies in the X block. The column part uk comes from X (the same column as before). The row part extends across the full augmented matrix: (vkT∣wkT), where vkT comes from the X block and wkT from the In block.

Since rank(X)=n, all n rows are covered by the n pivots. The In block is completely transformed. The decomposition gives:

(X∣In)=U(V∣W)

Comparing both sides:

  • Left part: X=UV ✓ (same as before)
  • Right part: In=UW

Step 3: From UW=In (both n×n), Proposition 4.3 gives WU=In.

Therefore U is invertible with U−1=W.

Step 4: Augmented cross-filling (vertical).

Similarly, apply cross-filling to (XIn) using the same pivots. At each step, the row part vkT comes from X. The column part extends: (ukgk), where uk comes from X and gk from In.

Since all n columns are covered, the decomposition gives:

(XIn)=(UG)V

This gives In=GV. By Proposition 4.3: VG=In.

Therefore V is invertible with V−1=G.

Step 5: X=UV with both U,V invertible, so:

X−1=V−1U−1=GW

Done! ∎

Example

Example 4.1: Finding an Inverse via Augmented Cross-Filling

Let X=(2111). Since rank(X)=2, it should be invertible.

Step 1: Cross-fill X at pivots (1,1) and (2,2).

Pivot (1,1)=2: row =(2,1), column =(21).

R1=12(21)(21)=(2111/2)

Remainder: X−R1=(0001/2).

Pivot (2,2)=1/2:

R2=11/2(01/2)(01/2)=(0001/2)

Product form: U=(2011/2), V=(11/201).

Step 2: Augmented cross-filling of (X∣I2).

Apply the same pivots to (21101101).

Pivot (1,1)=2: row =(2,1,1,0), column =(21).

Peel off: 12(21)(2,1,1,0)=(211011/21/20)

Remainder: (000001/2−1/21)

Pivot (2,2)=1/2: row =(0,1/2,−1/2,1).

So: V=(11/201), W=(1/20−12).

Verify: UW=(2011/2)(1/20−12)=(1001)=I2 ✓

Step 3: X−1=V−1U−1.

U−1=W=(1/20−12), V−1=(1−1/201).

X−1=V−1U−1=(1−1/201)(1/20−12)=(1−1−12)

Verify: X⋅X−1=(2111)(1−1−12)=(1001) ✓

Remark

The Augmented Cross-Filling and Classical Row Reduction

The augmented cross-filling technique is analogous to the classical method of finding X−1 by row-reducing (X∣I)→(I∣X−1). But here, the inverse emerges as a by-product of the cross-filling decomposition X=UV, and the proof of invertibility relies on the projection-theoretic Corollary 4.2 rather than on row-reduction theory.

5. Summary and Looking Ahead ​

::: success Key Theorems from This Lecture

  1. The Discovery (§1): Cross-filling a projection produces rank-1 projections that are mutually annihilating.

  2. VU=I Automatic (Theorem 2.1): If P=UV with P2=P and U,V full rank, then VU=I.

  3. Rank = Trace (Theorem 3.1): For any projection P, rank(P)=trace(P).

  4. Rank-1 Characterization (Corollary 4.1): Any two of {rank=1,trace=1,A2=A} imply the third.

  5. UV=I Forces Square (Corollary 4.2): If UV=I with U thin and V fat (or equal size), both must be square with VU=I.

  6. Full Rank ⟺ Invertible (Theorem 4.4): An n×n matrix is invertible if and only if it has rank n. :::

Looking Ahead to Lecture 9 ​

In this lecture, we proved that cross-filling a projection produces mutually annihilating rank-1 projections. But cross-filling is just one way to decompose a projection.

::: question Does this generalize?

Suppose we write a projection as any sum P=R1+⋯+Rk (not necessarily from cross-filling), with ∑rank(Ri)≤rank(P).

Must each Ri still be a projection? Must they still satisfy RiRj=0? :::

The answer is yes — this is the projection decomposition theorem, the central result of Lecture 9. This leads to:

  • Compatible families of projections
  • Powerful decomposition methods (inner cross-filling, diagonal cross-filling)
  • The foundation for spectral decomposition

Exercises ​

Problem

Exercise 1: Verify VU=I

For the projection P=12(1111):

(a) Find a cross-filling decomposition P=UV.

(b) Compute VU and verify it equals I1.

(c) Check that rank(P)=trace(P).

Problem

Exercise 2: Rank-1 Characterization

For each matrix, check which two of the three conditions hold, then deduce the third:

(a) A=(1/32/31/32/3) (given: rank = 1, trace = 1)

(b) B=(1−100) (given: rank = 1, B2=B)

(c) C=(1/21/21/21/2) (given: C2=C, trace = 1)

Problem

Exercise 3: One-Sided Inverse

Let F=(1−101) and V=(1101).

(a) Verify that FV=I2.

(b) Without computing VF directly, explain why Proposition 4.3 guarantees VF=I2.

(c) Now verify VF=I2 by direct computation.

Problem

Exercise 4: Full Rank and Invertibility

Determine which matrices are invertible using the rank criterion (Theorem 4.4):

(a) A=(1236)

(b) B=(1237)

(c) C=(102011003)

For invertible ones, find the inverse using augmented cross-filling.

Problem

Exercise 5: Trace and Rank

(a) Find a 3×3 projection with rank=2 and trace=2.

(b) Prove: if P is a projection and trace(P)=0, then P=0.

(c) Prove: if P is a projection and trace(P)=n (where P is n×n), then P=In.

Problem

Exercise 6 (Challenge)

Let A be a full-rank square matrix (n×n, rank(A)=n). Suppose A=R1+R2+⋯+Rn is the cross-filling decomposition into rank-1 pieces.

Prove that A−1Ri is a projection for each i=1,…,n.

Hint: Left-multiply A=R1+⋯+Rn by A−1. What do you get on the left? Apply the discovery from Section 1.
